Price the Stream

MKTG 2080 — Week 11, Day 2 Professor Key

1. Exercise Overview

What Students Do

Students price a fictional streaming service (StreamLocal) using three pricing methods: cost-plus, competitive, and value-based. Each student works on their own device (phone or laptop), calculating individual prices for each method. After each method, there's a mandatory group discussion gate where students compare prices, defend their choices, and decide if they'd change their answer. After all three methods, students vote on which method their CFO should recommend to the board, then discuss that choice with their group.

Exercise Type
Pricing Calculator
Individual Device + Discussion Gates
Duration
~25–30 min
Setup
Individual device, groups of 2–5
Email Required
UC email (@uc.edu)
Password
VALUE3
Fictional Company
StreamLocal
Local/regional content streaming
Design Intent The tension: three legitimate methods produce three different prices. There IS no single right answer — but value-based pricing should produce the highest price, and the debrief should reveal WHY. Students who default to cost-plus are thinking like accountants. Students who land on value-based are thinking like marketers. The discussion gates force students to defend their numbers face-to-face rather than picking prices randomly. After hearing their group's logic, students must actively decide whether to stick with their choice or change it.
Section 1.5
What Students See: Screen-by-Screen Walkthrough
Quick Reference These screenshots show every screen in order, with sample student data filled in. Use this to preview the exercise without playing through it yourself.
Password Gate

Password is VALUE3 (given in class).

Join Screen

Name, UC email (validated @uc.edu or @mail.uc.edu), and group number.

Instructions

StreamLocal scenario overview — students are the pricing team for a fictional local streaming service.

Data Briefing

Key financials: $2.4M fixed annual costs, $3.50/mo variable, 50,000 target subscribers. Students reference these throughout.

Round 1: Cost-Plus Pricing

Students pick a markup %, see the live calculated price, and write a justification.

Round 1: STOP — Group Discussion

Discussion gate: share prices, record what the group chose, decide if they'd change.

Round 2: Competitive Pricing

Students pick a target margin %, see the resulting price. Formula shown: Price = Variable Cost ÷ (1 - Margin%).

Round 2: STOP — Group Discussion

Same discussion gate pattern. Students record group perspectives and whether they'd change.

Round 3: Value-Based Pricing

Students set a price based on customer willingness to pay — no formula, pure judgment.

Round 3: STOP — Group Discussion

Final discussion gate before the method comparison.

Which Method Wins?

Students vote for one pricing method (Cost-Plus, Competitive, or Value-Based) and discuss as a group.

Method Vote (filled)

After voting, groups write their recommendation for which method to use and why.

Results Comparison

Side-by-side view of all three prices. Students see how different methods yield different prices for the same product.

Final Recommendation

Students choose one final price and write a justification (min 40 chars). This is the key deliverable.

Summary (top)

Complete submission summary: student info, three prices, method vote, and group recommendation.

Summary (bottom)

Final price, justification, and participation confirmation. Webhook fires here.

2. The Math — Complete Answer Key

Data Given to Students

  • Fixed costs: $2,400,000/year
  • Variable cost per subscriber: $3.50/month
  • Target: 50,000 subscribers in Year 1
  • Competitor prices: Netflix Ads $7.99, Hulu $9.99, Disney+ $9.99, Peacock $7.99, ESPN+ $11.99, Regional sports $20–30
  • Customer research: 60% pay $5–8, 25% pay $9–12, 10% pay $13–16, 5% pay $17+

Round 1: Cost-Plus Pricing

Price = Variable Cost + (Fixed Cost ÷ Subscribers ÷ 12) + Markup

Step-by-Step Calculation

  • Monthly fixed cost per subscriber: $2,400,000 ÷ 50,000 ÷ 12 = $4.00
  • Base cost per subscriber: $3.50 + $4.00 = $7.50
  • Apply markup at different levels below
Markup Price Monthly Revenue (50K subs) Annual Revenue
20% $9.00 $450,000 $5,400,000
30% $9.75 $487,500 $5,850,000
40% $10.50 $525,000 $6,300,000
50% $11.25 $562,500 $6,750,000
75% $13.13 $656,250 $7,875,000
100% $15.00 $750,000 $9,000,000
Teaching Note: Most students will pick 30–50% markup because it "feels fair." The right question isn't what markup feels fair — it's whether the resulting price matches what customers will pay. This is where the debrief becomes crucial.

Round 2: Competitive Pricing

Your Price = Competitor Price ± Positioning Adjustment

Step-by-Step Calculation

This formula only considers variable costs, NOT fixed costs. This is the trap.

Margin Price Analysis
30% $5.00 $5.00 × 50K × 12 = $3M annual. Fixed costs alone = $2.4M, leaving only $600K for variable costs. Variable cost = $3.50 × 50K × 12 = $2.1M. MASSIVE LOSS.
40% $5.83 $5.83 × 50K × 12 = $3.5M. Still short of the $2.4M fixed + $2.1M variable.
50% $7.00 $7.00 × 50K × 12 = $4.2M. Minus $2.4M fixed = $1.8M left. Variable = $2.1M. STILL SHORT.
60% $8.75 $8.75 × 50K × 12 = $5.25M. Minus $2.4M fixed = $2.85M for variable. Variable = $2.1M. First profitable option. Profit: $750K.
70% $11.67 $11.67 × 50K × 12 = $7M. Solidly profitable.
Critical Teaching Moment Students anchor against a competitor and set a price above, below, or at parity. The trap: anchoring against the cheapest competitor (Netflix at $6.99) drags the price down and ignores StreamLocal's unique value. The best prices anchor against ESPN+ ($10.99) or FuboTV ($17.99) — positioning StreamLocal as the smart local alternative. Ask: "Which competitor you anchor against reveals what you think StreamLocal is competing with. What does your anchor say about your strategy?"

Round 3: Value-Based Pricing

No formula — students first pick a target WTP segment (with live subscriber math), then set their price and defend the trade-off. The exercise surfaces the STP connection: who are you designing this price for?

Smart Pricing Anchors

  • Targeting the 60% ($5–8/mo) → ~30,000 subscribers: Price at $7–7.99 — maximum reach, minimum revenue per sub. Big bet on volume.
  • Targeting the 25% ($9–12/mo) → ~12,500 subscribers: Price at $10.99–$11.99 — anchored just above ESPN+. Best balance of reach and margin for most groups.
  • Targeting the 10% willing to pay $13–16: Price at $14.99 (premium local content, positioned against regional sports apps at $20–30)
Teaching Note: The new exercise forces students to pick a segment FIRST, then set the price — and shows them how many subscribers they're leaving behind. The "right" answer depends on which segment they target. Students who price at $7.99 are maximizing reach; students at $12–15 are betting on sports enthusiasts. NEITHER IS WRONG — but the exercise should surface: did they make a conscious choice, or just pick a number? The debrief question: "Why are you willing to walk away from 37,500 subscribers?"

Strong Justification Example

"We priced at $12.99 because we're targeting the 25% willing to pay $9–12 plus capturing some of the 10% willing to pay $13–16. Local sports is StreamLocal's differentiator (78% most valued), and there's no direct competitor at this price point — ESPN+ is $11.99 for national sports, regional apps are $20+. We sit in the gap."

3. The Reveal — What Happens When You Compare Methods

New: Method Vote After all 3 pricing rounds, students enter the "Which Method Wins?" screen. Here, they play CFO: they vote for ONE method (Cost-Plus, Competitive, or Value-Based) to recommend to the board. Then they discuss with their group and record the group's recommended method + reasoning (min 30 characters). Expected pattern: most groups will vote Value-Based after comparing all three methods. Strong debrief question: "Why did most of you pick value-based? What does that tell you about how real companies should think about pricing?"

Typical Pattern Across Groups

Method Typical Range What It Reveals
Cost-Plus $9.75–$11.25 Safe middle range. Ignores customer willingness to pay.
Competitive $5.00–$11.67 Widest range. Some clearly unprofitable. Many students don't realize they're losing money.
Value-Based $7.99–$14.99 Depends on segment targeted. Best prices reflect strategic customer choice.
Key Debrief Insight Cost-plus starts from your costs. Competitive starts from your rivals. Value-based starts from the CUSTOMER'S perception. Each step up the ladder moves the anchor closer to what actually matters in marketing. That's why value-based pricing is the goal of marketing — it puts the customer at the center of the pricing decision.

4. Concept Coverage Map

Explicitly Tested

Concept Exercise Round How Tested
Cost-Plus Pricing Round 1 Students calculate using the formula, choose markup level
Competitive Pricing Round 2 Students calculate and discover the fixed-cost trap
Value-Based Pricing Round 3 Students set price from customer research
Customer Segmentation Round 3 Students choose which segment to target
Fixed vs Variable Costs Rounds 1–2 Cost structure drives different results

Implicitly Surfaced

Concept Where It Surfaces
5 Cs of Pricing Group decision requires considering all 5 Cs from Tuesday
Competitive Positioning Value-based price must be defensible vs competitors
Price Anchoring Competitor prices anchor student expectations

5. Professor Guidance for Discussion Questions

Q1: "Which method produced the lowest price?"
Listen for: Cost-plus or competitive. If a group says value-based, ask why — they likely targeted a specific segment and can articulate the trade-off. If they say cost-plus, ask: "Does your customer care what it costs you to make this?"
Follow-up: "Why does starting from costs produce a lower price than starting from the customer?"
Q2: "Which method produced the highest?"
Listen for: Value-based. If not, the student likely targeted the wrong segment or left margin too conservative.
Follow-up: "What does it mean that the customer thinks the product is worth MORE than it costs to make?"
Q3: "Which method should StreamLocal actually use?"
Listen for: Value-based, but anchored to competitive reality. Pure value pricing without competitive awareness is arrogance.
Best answer: "Value-based targeting the $9–12 segment, anchored between ESPN+ ($11.99) and regional sports apps ($20+). Use cost-plus as the floor to make sure we're profitable."
Connection to Tuesday: "This is the 5 Cs working together — Company Objectives sets the target, Costs set the floor, Competition sets the range, Customers set the ceiling, Channel determines if anyone else needs a cut."

6. Common Student Mistakes

  • Choosing 50% margin and assuming it's profitable Ask: "Walk me through the total costs for 50,000 subscribers. Does your price cover everything?"
  • Pricing value-based at $5–6 to "get the most customers" Ask: "Can you cover your costs at that price? What are you actually optimizing for?"
  • Ignoring competitor data in value-based Ask: "Would you pay $14.99 for StreamLocal when Netflix with Ads is $7.99?"
  • All group members picking the same price Ask: "If cost-plus and value-based give you the same number, what does that tell you about how you're thinking about pricing?"
  • Defending cost-plus as "safest" Ask: "Safe for whom? Is the customer part of this equation?"

7. If a Group Finishes Too Fast

Extension 1: Exclusive Content Deal

"StreamLocal just signed an exclusive deal for your city's NBA team. Reprice using value-based. How much does the price change? Why?"

Expected insight: Exclusive local sports dramatically increases value perception. Students should reprice upward, likely to $15–18 range for the premium segment.

Extension 2: Competitive Response

"A competitor launches 'CityStream' at $6.99. Which pricing method helps you respond fastest? Which gives you the most room to absorb the hit?"

Expected insight: Value-based pricing gives you room because your price is anchored to value, not costs. Cost-plus forces you to choose between margin or price cut.

8. Facilitation Timeline

  • 0:00–2:00 Setup & Join Password entry, individual email entry (@uc.edu or @mail.uc.edu), group number collection, names entered on individual devices
  • 2:00–4:00 Instructions & Brand Intro Students review exercise overview and StreamLocal brand context
  • 4:00–6:00 Data Briefing Review Cost data, competitors, customer research. No calculations yet.
  • 6:00–10:00 Round 1: Cost-Plus (Phase A + B) Phase A: Student picks markup and writes justification. Phase B: STOP — Compare with Group: students share their prices, record what most chose and why (min 20 chars), indicate if they'd change (Yes/No + reason if Yes)
  • 10:00–14:00 Round 2: Competitive (Phase A + B) Same pattern: individual margin selection → group discussion gate → continue
  • 14:00–18:00 Round 3: Value-Based (Phase A + B) Individual free-price selection + justification → group discussion gate → continue
  • 18:00–21:00 Which Method Wins? CFO scenario: student votes for one method (Cost-Plus, Competitive, or Value-Based), then discusses with group and records group's recommended method + reasoning (min 30 chars)
  • 21:00–24:00 Results Review Student sees their 3 individual prices on results screen, sees group recommendation
  • 24:00–27:00 Summary & Final Reflection Debrief reflection question, exercise completion (webhook fires, email logged)
  • 27:00–30:00 Class Debrief Full class discussion using debrief questions and method vote patterns

9. Participation Tracking

Logistics: Email, Button & Webhook UC emails are collected at join screen for automated participation credit. When a student is ready to submit, they click "Submit & Record Participation →" — this fires a webhook containing their completion data (timestamp, group, email, prices, final recommendation, etc.) and lands them on the "Participation Recorded" summary screen. This can be connected to n8n → Canvas integration for automatic participation credit. Password: VALUE3 (unchanged).

What Gets Tracked

  • UC Email: Collected at join (@uc.edu or @mail.uc.edu)
  • Group Number: For grouping and discussion tracking
  • Completion: Webhook fires on completion with timestamp
  • Group Votes: Recorded group method choice and reasoning

Webhook Configuration

Configure webhook URL in exercise admin panel. Currently set to placeholder. When connected to n8n + Canvas, participation credit is automatically logged for each student email.

10. Connection to Tuesday (5 Cs of Pricing)

The summary screen asks: "Which of the 5 Cs from Tuesday most influenced your pricing decision today?"

Expected Answers

  • Costs: If they anchored to cost-plus. Not wrong, but incomplete.
  • Competition: If they priced relative to Disney+/ESPN+. Good defensive thinking.
  • Customers: If they used survey data as primary input. Best answer for marketing mindset.

Best Complete Answer

"All of them — but Customers drove the final number because we used value-based pricing, and Competition set the boundaries. We asked what the market would bear and where competitors sit, then used Costs only as a floor to ensure we're profitable."

Professor Note This is the moment to reinforce the difference between accounting and marketing. Accountants start with costs. Marketers start with customers and use costs as a constraint, not a driver.
For professor use only. Do not distribute to students.
